ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese broken rice (com tam) with pork ribs. Mapped to broader rice dish category FOODON_00002473 as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for com tam. A0EJW (rice-based composite dish) is the closest defensible broader category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for Vietnamese broken rice dish. SNOMED CT coverage for Southeast Asian composite dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of broken rice with five-spice grilled pork ribs and egg as typically served at a Vietnamese hawker stall weighs approximately 350g.

nutrient_source.title: Cơm Tấm Sườn Cọng (Broken Rice with Pork Ribs) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Cơm Tấm Sườn Cọng (Broken Rice with Pork Ribs), cr_id G00010565, confidence 0.95. All per_100g values preserved from the HPB record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese broken rice (com tam) as a composite dish. GI of 72 is estimated from white rice (jasmine/broken rice type) per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), which reports white rice GI range of 64–89 with a central estimate of ~72 for Southeast Asian white rice preparations. The pork ribs and egg components have negligible GI contribution. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (72) × carbohydrate per serving (25.19g/100g × 350g serving = 88.2g carbohydrate) / 100 = 63.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231mg/100g; estimated 808mg per 350g serving, partially offset by potassium 327mg/100g (1145mg per serving) and dietary fibre 3.7g/100g
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request fish sauce dipping sauce on the side and use sparingly, as the dish's sodium content of approximately 808mg per serving approaches one-third of the 2400mg daily limit; the high potassium content (1145mg per serving) provides a meaningful offset, but pickled vegetable accompaniments and marinade sauces should be minimised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI broken rice (GI ~72) contributing 25.2g carbohydrate/100g with a glycemic load of approximately 63.5 per standard serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size significantly (ideally to 150–200g) and pair the dish with additional non-starchy vegetables to slow glucose absorption, as the high-GI broken rice base produces a substantial glycemic load that is likely to cause postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Pork ribs contribute saturated fat (data not available from HPB source); sodium 231mg/100g; cholesterol reported as 0mg/100g in HPB source (likely underreported for a pork-and-egg dish)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation due to the pork rib component which typically contributes saturated fat; sodium intake should be managed by avoiding fish sauce dipping sauce and requesting reduced marinade, and the dish should not be consumed daily given the refined carbohydrate and animal fat load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork ribs are high in arachidonic acid (omega-6); high-GI rice base promotes pro-inflammatory glycemic load; dish lacks meaningful omega-3 content
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should limit frequency of consumption of this dish, as the pork rib component is rich in arachidonic acid which amplifies inflammatory cytokine production, and the high-GI broken rice base further promotes inflammatory mediators through glycemic load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Broken Rice with Five-Spice Grilled Pork Ribs and Egg (Cơm Tấm Sườn Cọng Ngũ Vị) is a Vietnamese rice dish built on a base of broken rice — the fragmented grains (tấm) that were historically a by-product of rice milling and became a staple of Southern Vietnamese cuisine, particularly in Ho Chi Minh City. The dish is widely served at Vietnamese hawker stalls in Singapore. The pork ribs (sườn cọng) are marinated in a five-spice (ngũ vị hương) blend of star anise, cloves, cinnamon, Sichuan pepper, and fennel seeds combined with fish sauce, garlic, lemongrass, and sugar, then grilled over charcoal or a gas grill to produce a caramelised, smoky exterior with a tender interior. The broken rice is steamed and served as a soft, slightly sticky base. A fried or steamed egg accompanies the dish, along with pickled daikon and carrot (đồ chua), sliced cucumber, tomato, and a small bowl of nước chấm (fish sauce dipping sauce sweetened with sugar and lime). Per 100g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5g protein, 4.21g fat, 25.19g carbohydrate, 3.7g dietary fibre, and 231mg sodium, with notable potassium (327mg), calcium (54mg), magnesium (40mg), and iron (2.55mg). The high-GI broken rice base (GI ~72) makes this dish a high glycemic load meal at standard serving sizes, requiring portion control for individuals managing blood glucose. The sodium content is moderate but can increase substantially with fish sauce dipping sauce. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans and is not certified halal due to pork content.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010565) with confidence 0.95. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous white rice entry, which is the closest approved GI source. Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown not reported in the HPB source record.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, vitamin breakdown, and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese broken rice dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (G00010565) as of 2026-04-16. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; this value is preserved as-is from the approved source, though it may be an underestimate for a dish containing pork ribs and egg — flagged for follow-up review. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white rice (Southeast Asian preparation) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Glycemic load per serving calculated as GI × available carbohydrate per serving / 100 = 72 × 88.2g / 100 = 63.5. The dish is not halal-certified due to pork content. Chinese names provided are descriptive translations; no standardised Chinese name for this specific Vietnamese dish variant has been identified in an approved source. Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names are descriptive translations as no standardized names were found for this Vietnamese dish in these languages.
